FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes prospective cohort data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 and Year 3 follow-up, N = 9799, 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using linear and logistic regression to examine cyberbullying victimization (Year 2) and mental health/substance use outcomes (Year 3).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Lancet Regional Health - Americas (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable (time on SM, texting, named platform, SMAQ, MPIQ, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. The sole exposure is cyberbullying victimization/perpetration. Total recreational screen time was measured but used only as a covariate in a sensitivity analysis (no estimate pairing a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait outcome). Step 2: Not reached. Per binding clarification, cyberbullying alone fails FT-IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient.

notes: Study focuses on cyberbullying victimization and perpetration as exposures predicting mental health and substance use. Screen time is measured and used as a covariate in sensitivity analyses only, not as a focal IV/DV. No phone/SM-specific variable carries its own coefficient paired with an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Year 3 of the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (2019-2021), N = 10,305, 12-13 year-olds, 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using modified Poisson regression and multivariable linear regression on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Sleep Health (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 returns a hit. Table 4 reports coefficients for individual bedtime screen modalities paired with sleep outcomes (individual-trait-level): 'Talk on the phone or text' (OR 1.11, 95% CI 1.05-1.18 for sleep disturbance; B = -0.20, 95% CI -0.24 to -0.15 for sleep duration) and 'Spend time online on social media (e.g., Facebook)' (OR 1.08, 95% CI 1.02-1.15; B = -0.25, 95% CI -0.29 to -0.20). These are phone/SM-specific variables as IV paired with sleep outcomes as DV.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates reported throughout Tables 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7: regression coefficients (B), odds ratios (OR), incidence rate ratios (IRR) for associations between bedtime screen use modalities and sleep outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed; multiple quantitative results reported (see FT-IC3 evidence).

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 requires a longitudinal design. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper examines bedtime screen use behaviors (including phone/text and social media) as predictors of sleep disturbance and sleep duration in cross-sectional ABCD Year 3 data. Also examines sociodemographic predictors of bedtime screen use. The phone/text and social media items in Table 4 qualify under Step 1 of FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Year 3 of the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (2019-2022), N = 10,048, 12-13 year-olds across 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple linear regression on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatric Research (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 returns a hit. Table 3 reports B = 0.82 (95% CI 0.55, 1.10) for parental screen time modeling predicting problematic social media use (SMAQ score as DV) and B = 1.31 (95% CI 1.01, 1.65) for parental screen time modeling predicting problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ score as DV). SMAQ and MPIQ are qualifying phone/SM variables. Parenting practices (parental screen time modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, monitoring, limiting) qualify as individual-trait-level on the other side (parenting practices / family functioning). Multiple other parenting practice predictors also show significant coefficients for SMAQ and MPIQ outcomes throughout Table 3.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients (B with 95% CI and p-values) reported in Table 3 for associations between six media parenting practice categories and problematic social media use (SMAQ), video game use (VGAQ), and mobile phone use (MPIQ) outcomes, as well as total screen time.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed; multiple quantitative results reported (see FT-IC3 evidence).

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 requires a longitudinal design. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Study examines six media parenting practice categories as predictors of adolescent total screen time and problematic social media (SMAQ), video game (VGAQ), and mobile phone (MPIQ) use. SMAQ and MPIQ are qualifying phone/SM variables as DVs. Parenting practices qualify as individual-trait-level (family functioning/parenting) on the IV side. Video game use (VGAQ alone) would not independently qualify but the paper also measures SMAQ and MPIQ, so IC2 is MET via those outcomes.
